preamble: The voiceless glottal fricative /h/ can undergo a phonetic transformation to the voiceless bilabial plosive /p/ through processes of fortition and assimilation. This shift is typically conditioned by the phonetic environment, often occurring in the presence of neighboring bilabial sounds.
phoneticEffects: Fortition (fricative to plosive), Place Assimilation (glottal to bilabial)

examples.note: The glottal fricative /h/ changes to a bilabial plosive [p] when immediately preceded by a bilabial nasal /m/.
The /h/ in the affix 'ma-hu' undergoes regressive assimilation to /p/ when attached to root adjectives ending in /m/ or /p/.
The /h/ in the affix 'pa-hu' undergoes regressive assimilation to /p/ when attached to root adjectives ending in /m/ or /p/.

sources: Nevins, A. (2011). Phonological Abstraction in The Mental Lexicon. UC San Diego.

Journal of Applied Linguistics. (December 09, 2024). The process of changing one sound into another sound i.e., sound [h] to [p] as the result of attaching the multiple affixations ma-hu to root adjectives is called regressive assimilation.
Journal of Applied Linguistics. (July 01, 2025). This sound change, from [h] to [p], that occurs when attaching pa-hu to root adjectives, is known as regressive assimilation.
